primary_diagnosis.name: Severe pediculosis capitis with secondary excoriation and early impetiginisation

pathophysiology_summary: Head lice feed on scalp blood, and sensitisation to louse saliva produces intense pruritus; relentless scratching has caused excoriations with early serosanguinous/golden crusting (secondary bacterial colonisation) and reactive posterior cervical lymphadenopathy. Diagnosis is confirmed by visualising live motile lice and firmly-cemented viable nits on proximal hair shafts (behind ears/nape). Discriminators: seborrhoeic scale is easily detached and lacks live insects; tinea capitis shows scaling/broken hairs/alopecia and often boggy inflammation, not motile lice; contact dermatitis lacks lice/nits and a defined external trigger. Management is a topical pediculicide (e.g., permethrin) applied correctly with a mandatory repeat at 7–10 days to kill newly-hatched nymphs, wet-combing, itch control, a short topical antibiotic to impetiginised areas, and simultaneous treatment of affected household contacts plus environmental measures. The safety-net is for progressing secondary infection (spreading hot redness, pus, fever).

differential.evidence_basis: Visualised live lice and cemented nits with itch/excoriation.
Scaly itchy scalp, but scale detaches easily and there are no live insects.
Would show scaling/broken hairs/alopecia, not motile lice.
Excoriated scalp can superinfect — screen for spreading redness/pus/fever.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Confirm lice, screen for secondary infection, and manage the household/re-treatment cycle.

differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed lice; early crusting only, no spreading infection/fever.
Early localised crusting; treat topically and safety-net for progression.
Working diagnosis: pediculicide + repeat at 7-10 days, wet-combing, itch control, topical antibiotic to crusted areas, household treatment; infection safety-net.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection of the scalp (golden crusting progressing to hot swollen spreading redness/pus)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Impetiginised excoriations can progress to cellulitis, especially with continued scratching.

red_flags.description: Systemic features (fever) or spreading neck redness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate significant secondary infection needing systemic antibiotics.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not advising the mandatory repeat pediculicide at 7-10 days, leading to reinfestation from hatched nymphs.
Treating only the child, allowing immediate reinfection.
Not screening/treating secondary bacterial infection of the excoriated scalp.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Assessing depth of any secondary infection
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prescribe pediculicide + repeat, wet-combing, itch control and topical antibiotic to crusted areas; treat household contacts; urgent review if secondary infection spreads or fever develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Single-dose treatment without repeat, ignoring contacts, or missing secondary infection.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Pediculosis capitis: topical pediculicide (e.g., permethrin) with a repeat application in 7-10 days, wet-combing, treat close contacts, and manage secondary infection if present.
